title: Harappan town in Gujarat, Hormuz tensions, RBI’s growth optimism: Today’s Premium reads
content:
A recreated Harappan city at Lothal, the renewed US-Iran confrontation over the Strait of Hormuz, and lessons from the Agra Summit 25 years on. Plus, why India needs to rethink its textile export strategy, what the RBI isn’t saying about inflation and growth, the suspension controversy around Himachal Police’s award-winning orchestra, and why PMOS is emerging as a major health concern for young women: Here are today’s Premium reads.
Visitors to Lothal will soon be able to walk through reconstructed Harappan streets, dockyards and marketplaces as part of the Rs 4,000-crore National Maritime Heritage Complex. Divya A reports how the project aims to bring the Indus Valley Civilisation to life through immersive exhibits while tracing 4,500 years of India’s maritime history.
Iran strikes threaten to unravel a fragile truce, the crisis is once again centred on the Strait of Hormuz. Shubhajit Roy explains what both sides have learnt from the conflict, why the waterway remains critical, and what renewed hostilities could mean for India.
25 years after the Agra Summit, Ajay Bisaria, who was private secretary to Prime Minister Atal Bihari Vajpayee and later became India’s High Commissioner to Pakistan, spoke to The Indian Express and shared his thoughts on lessons from the failed Summit and engaging with Pakistan in the current state of affairs.
India once dominated the global textile trade, but today it accounts for just 3% of apparel exports. Ashok Gulati and Sulakshana Rao argue that the country must build globally competitive ecosystems and strengthen institutions if it hopes to become a leading apparel exporter once again.
The RBI has kept interest rates unchanged and maintained a neutral stance, but its latest policy leaves key questions unanswered. Ishan Bakshi argues that the central bank needs to clearly explain how it balances rising inflation risks, slowing growth, the rupee’s trajectory, and the potential impact of US Fed decisions.
Harmony of the Pines, as the orchestra of the Himachal Pradesh Police is called, has come a long way since it started 30 years ago as a small group of seven members, to “alleviate stress faced by the police force”. Now, in one of the rare discordant notes to strike the band, its in-charge and virtually its face, Inspector Vijay Kumar, has gone to court over his suspension on July 13, on charges of making money by monetizing his personal social media account, without disclosing the same to the police department. Saurabh Parashar reports.
From irregular periods and infertility to insulin resistance, anxiety and heart health, PMOS is far more than an ovarian disorder. With studies suggesting nearly one in five Indian women may be affected, this report explains why diagnoses are rising, why India faces a higher burden than the global average, and what doctors say women need to know about managing the condition beyond weight loss.

title: World Cup belongs to all of Spain, says match-winning Torres
content:
winning goal for Spain on Sunday (July 19, 2026) belonged to "47 million people, not just me or the 26 players".
The Barcelona forward scored in the second period of extra time as Spain finally broke down 10-man Argentina to win 1-0 at MetLife Stadium.
"Ultimately, the goal belongs to 47 million people — not just me or the 26 players," said Torres, referring to Spain's approximate population.
"It was written in the stars that we would win far away from our people, yet we made them feel as close as possible," added Torres, who was named player of the match despite coming off the bench.
"It's a huge sense of relief. I faced a lot of criticism during the World Cup, but destiny is written. Thank God, who always gives me the strength to keep going."
He admitted it had been tough to win Spain's second World Cup to add to their first triumph in 2010.
"At the end of the day, all finals are tough. When you're up against (Lionel) Messi, there's a certain nervousness, but we always believed in our football and in ourselves."

title: Rudi Garcia is not continuing as Belgium coach after reaching World Cup quarterfinals
content:
Rudi Garcia is not continuing as Belgium coach after failing to reach an agreement with the country’s soccer federation on a contract extension.
The Royal Belgian Football Association said Monday (July 20, 2026) that it will not renew Garcia’s deal beyond the end of this month, and that it is looking for a successor to the French coach who led the Belgian team to the World Cup quarterfinals, where it was beaten by eventual champion Spain.
“Garcia has unquestionably played a key role in restoring the Belgian Red Devils,” Belgium sports director Vincent Mannaert said. “He was appointed national coach in a challenging sporting and financial context. Thanks in particular to his commitment and experience, team cohesion was restored and a strong result was achieved at the most recent FIFA World Cup.”
Garcia was appointed Belgium coach in January 2025 as the successor to the fired Domenico Tedesco, who failed to revive the team during a relatively short tenure. Garcia, who coached several big clubs including Napoli, Marseille and Roma in his much traveled career, had never managed a national side.
He touted his achievements in his 18 months as Belgium coach, pointing to the team’s World Cup campaign and improved standing since he took over.
“I wish Belgium every success as they continue the generational transition that I was proud to help initiate,” Garcia said.

title: Press and play: On the FIFA World Cup 2026
content:
Spain’s second World Cup title, achieved after a narrow extra-time victory over Argentina in the final, was a triumph of the collective. The Luis de la Fuente-coached outfit conceded just one goal in eight matches, was unmatched in the centre of the pitch with playmaker and Golden Ball winner Rodri completing a record 790 passes, and exhibited diffused excellence up front by having seven different players score its 14 goals. So much so that even the individual brilliance of the teenaged Lamine Yamal was astutely subsumed within the whole. In fact, de la Fuente and Rodri had combined to deliver the 2024 European Championship too, with the latter adjudged the world’s best later that year. A second Ballon d’Or for the 30-year-old midfield metronome appears a certainty. The win also cemented Spain’s position as the pre-eminent footballing force of the first quarter of this century. Starting 2008, the country has won two of the five World Cups and three of the five Euros. Spain is the reigning women’s World Champion, and the assembly line of coaches it produces and players it exports make the nation a true powerhouse. Interestingly, after its maiden World Cup win in 2010, Spain had not gone past the round of 16 at three subsequent editions. The success in North America is thus a full-circle moment.
Another high point for the tournament was Lionel Messi’s enduring genius. Argentina’s quality in the final was dire and the behaviour of its players petulant, but that should not detract from the 39-year-old’s excellence in bringing his country to the cusp of defending its crown. Kylian Mbappe’s experience was bitter-sweet; he won the Golden Boot (10 goals) and became the all-time top-scorer in World Cups (22) but remained a mute spectator as Spain subjugated his star-studded France in the semifinal. Jude Bellingham inspired England to third place, the best performance for the nation since winning in 1966. But the summer sojourn laid bare the team’s continued lack of cutting edge after back-to-back runner-up finishes at Euro 2020 and 2024. The World Cup will also be etched in memory for a plethora of missteps, which governing body FIFA unfortunately condoned. By arbitrarily invoking immigration rules, and trying to interfere politically in on-field matters, the co-host U.S. pushed the envelope in undesirable ways. Commercially driven hydration breaks and the half-time show in the final were tedious and disruptive. Football should be allowed to flow freely, on and off the turf.

title: FIFA World Cup 2026 | A legend built without its decorated kings
content:
Somewhere between Ferran Torres’ extra-time winner and the trophy lift, 12 club badges got to claim a piece of history. Spain’s second World Cup title was stitched together by clubs, its academies and leagues spread across Europe, and the breakdown tells a story as interesting as the triumph itself.
LaLiga dominated the numbers, with 17 of the 26 players coming from the Spanish top division, a figure league president Javier Tebas was quick to hold up as proof of its strength. The Premier League supplied seven, while the Bundesliga and Ligue 1 contributed one each, reflecting how Spanish football has kept its biggest talents close to home even as the English top flight’s financial gravity pulls them elsewhere.
Barcelona led individual club representation with nine players, from Lamine Yamal and Pedri to Pau Cubarsi, underlining a squad heavily shaped by La Masia’s production line. Atletico Madrid, Athletic Bilbao, and Arsenal each sent three players. Mikel Merino, coming fresh off a Premier League title with the Gunners, carried that winning momentum into the World Cup as he scored the game-clinching goals against Portugal and Belgium.
Perhaps the most striking detail, though, was who was missing. For the first time in Spain’s World Cup history, not a single Real Madrid player featured in the squad, a remarkable absence for the nation’s most successful club. Marc Cucurella’s move to Real from Chelsea, confirmed during the tournament, only added to the irony.
Football has a way of humbling even its grandest institutions, and Real’s absence is proof enough. Spain didn’t need its most decorated club to become the champion again; it just needed 26 players from a dozen different dressing rooms.

title: FIFA World Cup 2026 | Messi leaves the World Cup as its most enduring presence
content:
For 120 minutes, Spain did what no other team at this World Cup had managed.
It found a way to keep Lionel Messi quiet.
The plan was simple in theory but brutally difficult in practice. Every time the 39-year-old drifted towards the right flank, another red shirt arrived. His every passing lane closed and every familiar slalom towards his left foot was met by bodies rather than space.
“The first idea was to keep Messi at bay,” Spain coach Luis de la Fuente admitted afterwards. He had learned his lesson when a 16-year-old Messi had terrorised his Sevilla team in an under-19 match in 2004.
But here, rather than man-marking the Argentine talisman, the Spanish coach looked at cutting the supply and isolating Messi. The Argentine captain managed only 15 touches in the first half and 54 across the entire final. There was no trademark curling finish, no defence-splitting pass, no decisive intervention.
The overwhelming majority of the 80,663 spectators had come hoping to witness another coronation. They wanted to see the man who had already given them one of football’s greatest endings add one final chapter by lifting a second World Cup.
Instead, the faithful watched him leave in tears.
It was here in MetLife in 2016, when Messi had missed in the Copa America final shootout against Chile and announced his international retirement before making a comeback 47 days later to complete his redemption arc.
Ten years later, he returned to the same stadium seeking one final crown, only for it to deny him again.
As Spain celebrated at the centre circle, Messi and his teammates walked towards the Argentina supporters gathered behind the southern goal. The applause that greeted them was as loud as anything heard all evening. Spain’s players, too, understood the moment.
Several, including Lamine Yamal, crossed the pitch to embrace Messi after the final whistle. Later, as Argentina climbed the podium to collect its runner-up medals, the new world champion formed a guard of honour. A few patted him on the back, Messi tried to smile, but the disappointment was hard to contain.
One match, however, cannot rewrite a career.
Messi leaves the World Cup as its most enduring presence with 34 appearances, 23 victories, 21 goals, 27 matches as captain, seven goals from outside the penalty area, 10 knockout-stage assists and, above all, the man who finally completed football’s greatest story by lifting the trophy in 2022. He remains the only player to score in every knockout round of a World Cup-winning campaign.
Spain may have denied him one final triumph, but no one can take away the imprint he has left on this tournament.
For generations to come, the World Cup’s story will be retold through many champions, but few names will echo as deeply as that of Lionel Messi’s.

title: Teen dies during Spain World Cup celebrations: local authorities
content:
A teenager died, and several people were injured during celebrations of Spain's victory in the World Cup after a fountain collapsed in the west of the country, local authorities and media said on Monday (July 20, 2026).
"The city hall of Ciudad Rodrigo expresses its sadness and presents its condolences for the death of a boy of 13," the municipality said in a Facebook post.
"What should have been a celebration of Spain's team in the football World Cup turned into tragedy," it said, adding that several others were injured.
"A few minutes after 12:30 a.m. local time, rescue services received calls over a fountain collapsing on numerous people," emergency services said in a post on X, adding that another youth had been injured.
Local media reported that the fountain collapsed under the weight of numerous people who had climbed onto it to celebrate Spain's first World Cup victory since 2010 in the football-mad nation.

title: Hyderabad stays awake as Spain’s FIFA World Cup triumph against Argentina sparks midnight celebrations
content:
Football fever gripped Hyderabad well past midnight as hundreds of fans gathered at People’s Plaza in Necklace Road despite intermittent rain, to watch Spain defeat Argentina in the FIFA World Cup final on a giant screen in the early hours of Monday (July 20, 2026). The open-air screening turned into a carnival of football, with cheers, fireworks and emotional celebrations keeping the fans in the city awake until dawn nearly.
People began arriving at the venue on the intervening night of Sunday and Monday, with the crowd steadily swelling as kick-off approached. Organisers had set up a large LED screen at the plaza, while a heavy police presence was deployed to manage the gathering and ensure smooth movement of traffic.
“We were planning to watch the match at our friend’s place, but then we saw on Instagram that there was a live screening at People’s Plaza, so we decided to come here,” said Mohd Abdul, an IT professional.
The atmosphere remained electric throughout the contest. With neither side finding the breakthrough during regulation time, every attacking move was met with loud cheers and collective gasps from the crowd. As the match entered extra time, tension among supporters was palpable.
The deadlock was finally broken at around 3.15 a.m. when Spain found the back of the net in extra time, triggering celebrations among Spanish supporters. Fireworks illuminated the sky as fans erupted in joy, while Argentina supporters were left visibly disappointed.
When the final whistle confirmed Spain’s victory shortly before 3.30 a.m., celebrations intensified. Hundreds of fans pulled out their mobile phones to capture the historic moment as another round of fireworks lit up the night sky. Many stayed back to watch the post-match trophy presentation, while others began making their way home.
“We wanted Argentina to win, so the result hurts. But watching a World Cup final with hundreds of fans, despite the rain, was an unforgettable experience. If this is Lionel Messi’s last World Cup, I’m glad I witnessed it like this,” said Shashank Reddy, a college student.
The simultaneous departure of hundreds of spectators led to brief traffic congestion around People’s Plaza and Necklace Road, though police personnel quickly cleared the bottlenecks and restored normal traffic flow.
The football celebrations were not confined to People’s Plaza. Across Hyderabad, several licensed bars, clubs and microbreweries hosted live screenings after the State government permitted extended operating hours for establishments for the FIFA World Cup final. Licensed outlets were allowed to remain open until 3 a.m., enabling fans to watch the title clash late into the night.

title: FIFA World Cup final: Argentina’s Scaloni admits Spain was better, praises his players
content:
Lionel Scaloni admitted Spain deserved to be crowned world champions but said he could not have asked for more from his Argentina players after their 1-0 extra-time defeat in the FIFA World Cup final on Sunday (July 19, 2026).
time despite playing the closing stages with 10 men after Enzo Fernandez’s stoppage-time red card, before Ferran Torres finally settled the contest.
“I feel sad,” Scaloni said. “They were better, that’s true, but I will have tremendous memories of what my players achieved. We are great in victory and must be so in defeat as well. Tonight, we showed that we know how to lose. We lost, and we accept it.”
The Argentina coach thanked the squad that had carried the nation to a second successive World Cup final after another remarkable tournament.
“Endless thanks to these players who once again took us to the final. If they give everything the way they did today, it sets a great example for the people and for our country. When you leave everything like that, it’s very hard to find fault with anything.”
Scaloni also paid tribute to Lionel Messi, who finished what is expected to be his final World Cup with eight goals after another outstanding campaign.
“He’s 39 years old now. It was crystal clear for me that he was going to play until he decided not to,” Scaloni said. “I hope everyone feels proud of him because he’s the best football player ever to set foot on a pitch. What he did during this World Cup was incredible.”
The 48-year-old then became visibly emotional while discussing his own future, with his contract due to expire later this year.
“I will talk to the president. I think it’s only fair that I take this time for myself,” he said. “In order to continue, you need to reset your mind. Creating a group like this again is very difficult.”
Scaloni paused, apologised to reporters and walked out of the press conference in tears, bringing an emotional end to Argentina’s reign as world champion.

title: Karmakar backs Pranati to end India’s CWG gymnastics medal wait
content:
As Glasgow prepares itself to host Commonwealth Games later this week, one person who couldn’t help but feel a little nostalgic is artistic gymnast
Dipa Karmakar. She won the bronze in the women’s vault when the event was held in the Scottish city in 2014 - her maiden international medal.
It marked the beginning of a remarkable journey that came to an end in 2024 when she retired after winning gold at Asian Championships.
Having transitioned into coaching, Dipa remains deeply connected to the sport and will be glued to the screens when the competition begins on Thursday. She will also be rooting for Pranati Nayak, who she believes has a good chance of finishing on the podium this time.
“We were together during the last two national camps. I was with the junior team at Asian Championships in China, while she was competing alongside Olympic and World Championship medallists,” Dipa said during an interaction facilitated by Sports Authority of India (SAI).
“She performed well and reached the final. I hope she has upgraded one of her vaults, and if she executes both vaults well, I believe she has a very good chance of winning a medal.”
Having said that, one also needs to keep in mind that the veteran gymnast has been struggling with injuries, especially after hurting her ankle during last year’s World Championships.
“I have gone through two ACL surgeries myself, so I know how difficult the comeback can be.
But I hope she returns even stronger. Commonwealth Games come only once every four years, so this is a huge opportunity,” Dipa added.
Besides Pranati, India will have several other gymnasts in action, including a few making their Commonwealth Games debut. While Dipa believes the event will be a huge opportunity for them, she is already looking ahead to the next Commonwealth Games, which will be held in Ahmedabad in 2030.
“I competed at 2010 Commonwealth Games in Delhi, and I know what hosting such an event means,” Dipa said. “This time, I hope India has its best-ever Commonwealth Games. We have four years to prepare, and if everyone continues working hard, I believe we can win many more medals.”
She is also hopeful of India getting the opportunity to host the 2036 Olympics, which she believes would transform the country’s sporting ecosystem.
“It would be a huge achievement, not only for athletes but for Indian sport as a whole,” said the Rio Olympian. “Hosting the Olympics means better infrastructure, foreign coaches, exposure trips, improved facilities and greater investment in sport.”
The ‘Produnova Girl’, as she is popularly known, is meanwhile delighted with the progress Indian gymnastics has made over the past decade. But once Commonwealth Games and Asian Games are over, she wants the current crop of gymnasts to turn their attention towards the 2028 Los Angeles Olympics.

title: Luis de la Fuente: The architect behind Spain's FIFA World Cup revival
content:
Spaniards appear to be particularly skilled at frustrating their opponents. Their PM, Pedro Sanchez, showed this on the geopolitical pitch, annoying Trump repeatedly over his refusal to let US use Spain’s airspace for the Iran war.
Weeks later, Luis de la Fuente, head of their national team, expressed similar faith in his ‘camaraderie’ theory to frustrate football’s superpowers — Portugal, Belgium, France and finally the ‘untouchables’,
Lionel Messi’s Argentina. Spain’s World Cup triumph marks the return of a footballing empire — one built not on the brilliance of superstars, but on the power and appeal of the collective.
Having conquered Europe in 2024, Spain’s Sunday victory in New York two years later completed a remarkable renaissance, confirming that the country’s football identity had evolved rather than simply returned.
based approach to suffocate the opposition into submission, kicked off by Luis Aragones, had earned Spain their first continental trophy in 2008, after 44 years.
Then, they conquered the world in 2010, riding atop Vicente del Bosque’s passing carousel. The European summit re-scaled in 2012 was the high-point, a delicious mix of passing creativity and penalty box sharpness. But then, Spain lost their way in a passing warp.
Caught in the maze of possession, Spain wallowed in the quagmire as Del Bosque’s men failed in 2014. In Qatar 2022, enjoying 75% possession, Luis Enrique’s boys failed to score in 120 minutes against Morocco! In walked Luis de la Fuente. As of Sunday, Spain are yet to lose any of their continental clashes since the 65-year old’s appointment, four years ago. La Roja won all seven games to claim the Euro 2024 scoring 15 goals. This time, they won seven out of eight with one draw, scored 14 and conceded just one. Leveraging a decade of nurturing Spain’s youth teams, he used his deep bonds with players he had nourished since their teenage years.
Unlike many modern international sides that revolve around one transcendent figure, Spain’s greatest strength was that no player was indispensable. Lamine Yamal dazzled, Rodri controlled, Pedri dictated tempo, Nico Williams stretched defences and Ferran Torres, a substitute, delivered the decisive goal. Yet none of them carried the burden alone. With the fate of the final still precariously poised, Rodri made way for Zubimendi for his first taste of the World Cup in the 99th minute. Ideas over icons.
The architect of this resurgence was De la Fuente himself. He coached Spain’s Under-19s to the European title in 2015, the Under-21s to the European Championship in 2019, and the Olympic side to silver in Tokyo before taking charge of the senior team in 2022. Many of the players who lifted the World Cup had already learned his theorems as teenagers. Players arrived in the senior squad fluent in De la Fuente’s language. The national team was simply the latest stage of a long developmental journey.
Euro 2024 offered the first glimpse of this transformation. Spain abandoned the stereotype of sterile possession that had come to define the latter years of the Xavi-Iniesta era (2008-2012). They added speed, verticality and athleticism.
Lamine Yamal and Nico Williams provided width and acceleration, while Rodri and Pedri ensured control in midfield. The European title was the foundation of a larger project that reached its logical conclusion on Sunday.

title: Messi suffers unwanted first in FIFA World Cup final as Spain silence Argentina icon
content:
Lionel Messi endured a rare off day in Argentina's FIFA World Cup 2026 final defeat to Spain, with the legendary forward failing to create a single chance for a teammate for the first time in his World Cup career.
The statistic underlined Spain's disciplined defensive display as Luis de la Fuente's side neutralised Argentina's biggest attacking threat on their way to a 1-0 extra-time victory at MetLife Stadium.
Messi, making his 34th World Cup appearance across six tournaments, had never previously started a
FIFA World Cup match without creating at least one chance for a teammate. That remarkable streak came to an end in the final as Spain successfully denied him the space and time that have defined his international career.
The 39-year-old was unable to produce the moments of brilliance that had carried Argentina to a second successive World Cup final. Twenty years after making his tournament debut, Messi saw his bid to become a two-time world champion end as Spain claimed their second FIFA World Cup title.
Spain's plan to contain the Argentine captain worked almost perfectly. Messi had just 15 touches in the opening half and finished the match with only 54, rarely receiving possession in dangerous areas.
His passing combinations failed to click, and he never found himself in a position to seriously threaten Spain's defence.
After the match, Spain coach Luis de la Fuente admitted stopping Messi had been the team's primary objective.
"The first idea was to keep Messi at bay," he said.
Spain dominated large portions of the contest, finishing with a 20-2 advantage in shots while completing 845 passes compared to Argentina's 433. Ferran Torres eventually settled the final 37 seconds into the second period of extra time, firing into the roof of the net after Nico Williams headed the ball into his path.
The victory secured Spain's first World Cup title since 2010 and extended La Roja's unbeaten international run to a European-record 38 matches.
For Messi, the defeat marked a disappointing end to an otherwise outstanding tournament. The Argentina captain led his nation back to the World Cup final with eight goals and four assists, while becoming the first player to start three FIFA World Cup finals.
He also finished the tournament with 125 international goals, second only to
Cristiano Ronaldo's 146, and made a record 34 World Cup appearances.
Following the final whistle, an emotional Messi was consoled by several Spain players before walking through a guard of honour formed by the newly crowned champions as Argentina collected their runners-up medals.
As Spain celebrated with the trophy, Messi left the field wearing his silver medal, his hopes of guiding Argentina to back-to-back World Cup titles having been ended by a Spanish side that succeeded where every other opponent had failed, keeping one of football's greatest creators from producing a single chance.

title: Why Marc Cucurella buried coin in pitch before Spain's World Cup final as 'secret' 2010 ritual comes to light
content:
match ritual moments before Spain's FIFA World Cup 2026 final against Argentina, and it has now emerged that the act was part of a 16-year-old superstition passed down by one of Spain's previous World Cup winners.
Spain defeated Argentina 1-0 after extra time at the New York New Jersey Stadium to lift the men's World Cup for the second time in their history, with substitute Ferran Torres scoring the decisive goal in the 106th minute.
While much of the attention after the final centred on Spain's triumph and Argentina's late collapse, footage captured shortly before kick-off revealed Cucurella quietly walking towards the touchline, removing an object from his sock and burying it beneath the turf.
The 'secret' lucky coin tradition
Initially, nobody knew what the Spain left-back had hidden in the grass.
The mystery was solved after the final by former Spain defender Joan Capdevila, who revealed that the object was a coin and that he had personally asked Cucurella to continue a superstition dating back to Spain's first World Cup triumph in 2010.
Capdevila, who started at left-back when Spain defeated the Netherlands in the 2010 World Cup final in Johannesburg, explained that he had buried a coin in the pitch before that match and encouraged Cucurella to repeat the ritual before facing Argentina.
Writing on X, Capdevila said: "And now I'll tell you a secret... In 2010, I buried a coin in the grass before starting the match... I've told it millions of times.
"I asked @cucurella3 to do the same yesterday...
"Marc, I love you so much. YOU ARE A WORLD CHAMPION!"
off.
Spain's lucky charm ends with another World Cup
Whether coincidence or superstition, the ritual appeared to work once again.
Spain controlled much of the final against defending champions Argentina before finally finding the breakthrough in extra time.
Ferran Torres struck in the 106th minute after being set up by Nico Williams, handing Luis de la Fuente's side a hard-earned 1-0 victory and securing Spain's second World Cup title, exactly 16 years after their first.
Cucurella was once again a key figure throughout the tournament, capping another successful international campaign after helping Spain win the European Championship two years earlier.
Argentina's hopes of forcing penalties had already suffered a major blow when Enzo Fernández was sent off in stoppage time of normal time after receiving a second yellow card for a reckless challenge on Pau Cubarsí. Tempers later boiled over after the final whistle, with Leandro Paredes also being dismissed following an altercation involving several Spain players.
Capdevila's own World Cup journey almost ended before it began
The story carried extra significance because Capdevila himself almost missed the final.
Earlier in the week, the 2010 World Cup winner revealed that his Electronic System for Travel Authorization (ESTA) application to enter the United States had been denied, reportedly because of a previous exhibition match he had played in Iran.
Capdevila publicly appealed to US President Donald Trump on social media, writing: "I NEED HELP @realDonaldTrump!"
"They just told me that I can't travel to the final with my kids because my ESTA has been denied.
"Can anyone help me with this? You have no idea how excited I was to be there with all my 2010 teammates and this team to cheer them on.
"I can't believe they're not letting me into the USA... and that I'll miss a moment like this with my kids who love soccer so much."
According to ESPN, US Customs and Border Protection later confirmed to the Associated Press that it had received a request regarding Capdevila's case and subsequently granted him permission to enter the country.
The former Villarreal defender arrived in New Jersey in time for the final and watched Spain lift the World Cup from the stands.
After the victory, he celebrated both Spain's triumph and his own successful journey by posting: "HOW WASN'T IT WORTH IT... I DESERVED TO BE HERE WITH MY FAMILY ENJOYING THIS MOMENT, AND JUSTICE HAS BEEN SERVED. Thanks to everyone who pushed for this to be possible, thanks to all of you from the bottom of my heart."
For Capdevila, the coin buried beneath the grass had already brought Spain luck once in 2010. Sixteen years later, after Cucurella repeated the same quiet ritual before kick-off, it ended with another World Cup trophy heading back to Spain.

title: Trump said same seven words to every Spain and Argentina player after World Cup final, claims lip reader
content:
United States President Donald Trump reportedly delivered the same congratulatory message to every Spain and Argentina player during the medal ceremony following the FIFA World Cup 2026 final, according to a professional lip reader.
Trump joined FIFA president Gianni Infantino on the podium after Spain defeated Argentina 1-0 after extra time at the New York New Jersey Stadium to claim their second World Cup title. Ferran Torres' 106th-minute winner settled a tense final that saw Argentina finish with 10 men after Enzo Fernández's second yellow card deep into stoppage time of normal time.
The medal presentation featured several world leaders alongside FIFA officials, with Trump greeting every player individually before presenting medals to the runners-up and the newly crowned champions.
Lip reader claims Trump repeated the same message
According to professional lip reader Nicola Hickling of LipReader Ltd, Trump stuck to an identical message as each player arrived on stage.
Mirror, Hickling claimed Trump shook hands with every player before telling them: "You are a champ."
Rather than holding lengthy conversations, the lip reader suggested the US President repeated the same words to each player while either placing the medal around their neck or briefly patting them on the chest before they moved along the presentation line.
President Donald J. Trump gives Spain's Ferran Torres (7) winners metal during the World Cup final soccer match between Spain and Argentina in East Rutherford, N.J., near New York, Sunday, July 19, 2026. (AP Photo/Julio Cortez)
World leaders joined medal ceremony
Trump attended the presentation alongside FIFA president Gianni Infantino, Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney and Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum following the conclusion of the tournament.
The leaders took part in the official medal presentation after Spain ended a memorable campaign by defeating defending champions Argentina in extra time.
Spain head coach Luis de la Fuente, left, is greeted by FIFA President Gianni Infantino and President Donald J. Trump during the presentation ceremony after the World Cup final soccer match between Spain and Argentina in East Rutherford, N.J., near New York, Sunday, July 19, 2026. (AP Photo/Julio Cortez)
Spain's victory also extended their unbeaten international run to 38 matches, the longest streak in international football history, as Luis de la Fuente's side completed another major triumph following their European Championship success.
Trump again becomes talking point after trophy presentation
Although the spotlight belonged to Spain's players, Trump again became one of the major talking points after the final whistle.
As Rodri prepared to lift the World Cup trophy alongside his teammates, television cameras showed Trump remaining on the podium during the celebrations before Gianni Infantino stepped in and appeared to usher him aside so Spain could enjoy the traditional trophy-lift photograph.
Trump then moved away from the centre of the stage while Spain celebrated with the World Cup trophy.
Spain players, from left, cheer alongside President Donald Trump and FIFA President Gianni Infantino at the conclusion of the World Cup championship final soccer match between Spain and Argentina in East Rutherford, N.J., Sunday, July 19, 2026. (AP Photo/Jacquelyn Martin)
The moment echoed his prominent involvement at the FIFA Club World Cup final earlier in the year, although on this occasion his participation in the celebrations was considerably shorter.
Final overshadowed by late controversy
The medal ceremony followed an eventful conclusion to the tournament.
Spain controlled much of the final before Ferran Torres broke the deadlock in the 106th minute of extra time.
Argentina's hopes had already suffered a major setback after Enzo Fernández was sent off in stoppage time of normal time for a reckless challenge on Pau Cubarsí, leaving Lionel Scaloni's side to contest extra time with 10 men.
After the final whistle, celebrations were briefly overshadowed by a mass confrontation involving players from both teams. Leandro Paredes was shown a straight red card after becoming involved in an altercation with Spain players, while Nahuel Molina was also among those at the centre of the post-match scuffles.

title: After beating Argentina, Spain return to Madrid with FIFA World Cup 2026 trophy
content:
Fresh off their FIFA World Cup 2026 triumph, Spain's national football team has returned home to a hero's welcome, with celebrations set to take over the streets of Madrid. The Royal Spanish Football Federation (RFEF) confirmed on Monday that the World Cup-winning squad had landed in the Spanish capital, while the players shared their excitement about celebrating the historic achievement with supporters.
In a post on X, the team wrote:
"Yes, yes, yes! The World Cup is finally here. The Spain squad has landed in Madrid... and we can't wait to celebrate with all of you!"
To mark the occasion, Spain announced a public victory celebration titled "Reverse of the World", which will take place on July 20 at the Cibeles Communications Palace in Madrid. The festivities are scheduled to begin at 6:00 PM local time, with supporters able to attend free of charge until the venue reaches capacity.
Spain end 16-year wait for World Cup glory
Spain secured their second FIFA World Cup title by defeating Argentina 1-0 after extra time in the final at New York New Jersey Stadium, claiming their first world crown since 2010.
winner, scoring in the 106th minute after connecting with a loose ball inside the penalty area following a cross from Pedro Porro and an assist from Nico Williams. La Roja dominated the contest throughout, enjoying 68% possession while firing 20 shots, including 11 on target, compared to Argentina's four attempts.
Goalkeeper Emiliano Martínez kept Argentina in the contest with a remarkable 11 saves, the most ever recorded in a FIFA World Cup final, but even his heroics were not enough to deny Spain. Argentina's task became even more difficult when Enzo Fernández was sent off early in extra time after receiving a second yellow card. Despite a late opportunity falling to Giuliano Simeone, the defending champions were unable to force penalties as Spain sealed a memorable victory.
